 {"id":"bd-1iuu","title":"Overhead: Implement adaptive tuner (e-process confidence sequence + evidence ledger)","description":"Implement the optional-but-recommended adaptive overhead tuner from spec §3.5.12 (Anytime-valid durability autopilot).

## Observations
Every verified SymbolRecord produces a Bernoulli observation:
- `X=1` record corrupt / invalid (hash mismatch, auth_tag fail, etc)
- `X=0` record ok

Also track erasures (missing symbols) during reads/replication.

## Monitor (Anytime-valid)
- Maintain an e-process monitor for hypotheses like:
  - `H0: p_corrupt <= p0` (budget per object class/tier)
  - optionally `H0: p_erase <= e0`
- When the monitor rejects, we must harden:
  - increase overhead_percent (and thus R)
  - optionally retroactively generate more deterministic repair symbols

Key property: optional-stopping safe (Ville).

## Policy Actions
- Increase overhead:
  - monotonic, capped by overhead_max
  - emits evidence ledger entry with:
    - reason (which monitor)
    - old/new overhead
    - scope (object class/tier)
    - timestamp/epoch
- Decrease overhead:
  - optional; only allowed under conservative loss and after strong evidence
  - must never cause under-provisioning for small K

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tuner exists, is deterministic, and emits evidence ledger entries.
- Unit tests simulate sequences where:
  - corruption rate > budget -> overhead increases
  - safe regime persists -> overhead stays stable (no thrash)
  - decrease policy (if implemented) is conservative and explainable","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:35.048190971Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:11:28.436593052Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T22:11:28.436571862Z","close_reason":"Adaptive tuner + evidence-ledger path already present in repair_symbols.rs (AdaptiveRedundancyPolicy, FailureRateMonitor e-process, deterministic evaluate path, retune ledger entries, and bd-1hi.30 unit/property/E2E-style tests).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1iuu","depends_on_id":"bd-2pcg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:54:38.929959033Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1iuu","depends_on_id":"bd-3lpo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:54:38.739048933Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1j3s","title":"Phase 5E: MVCC Concurrent Writers Integration","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:52:02.805851434Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:17.205661847Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","depends_on_id":"bd-1299","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:17.205596895Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","depends_on_id":"bd-14zc","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:15.970490229Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","depends_on_id":"bd-1xo1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:16.467358733Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","depends_on_id":"bd-3bql","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:16.961200214Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","depends_on_id":"bd-3uzh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:16.713438374Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","depends_on_id":"bd-kivg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:16.221273701Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1281,"issue_id":"bd-1j3s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# Phase 5E: MVCC Concurrent Writers Integration

## Purpose

This is the final phase of the storage stack wiring project. Phases 5A-5D establish the
single-writer path: schema in sqlite_master, DML through B-tree/pager, transactions through
pager commit/rollback. Phase 5E enables the **multi-writer path** — the core innovation of
FrankenSQLite that replaces SQLite's global WAL_WRITE_LOCK with page-level MVCC locking.

## Why this matters

Standard SQLite allows exactly one writer at a time (WAL_WRITE_LOCK at wal.c:3676). FrankenSQLite's
entire raison d'être is MVCC page-level versioning: multiple writers can proceed concurrently as
long as they touch different B-tree pages. This is what makes the project worth building.

The MVCC infrastructure is already fully implemented in `crates/fsqlite-mvcc/`:
- `ConcurrentRegistry` — tracks active concurrent sessions with write sets
- `InProcessPageLockTable` — sharded page-level exclusive locks (64 shards, CacheAligned)
- `SharedPageLockTable` — cross-process lock table (open-addressing, atomic CAS, rolling rebuild)
- `CommitIndex` — maps pages to latest CommitSeq for first-committer-wins validation
- `VersionStore` — bump-allocated arena with per-page version chains for snapshot reads
- `SsiState` — SSI rw-antidependency tracking (has_in_rw, has_out_rw, pivot detection)
- `GcScheduler` — frequency-adaptive garbage collection for old page versions
- `WriteCoordinator` — Native (tiny-marker) and Compatibility (WAL append) publish paths
- `RetryPolicy` — Beta-Bernoulli model for adaptive SQLITE_BUSY backoff

What's missing is wiring this infrastructure through Connection → Pager → TransactionHandle so that
`BEGIN CONCURRENT` actually engages MVCC page-level locking instead of falling through to
the serialized (global mutex) path.

## Architecture

```
Connection.execute_begin(CONCURRENT)
  → Pager.begin(TransactionMode::Concurrent)
    → ConcurrentRegistry.begin_concurrent(snapshot) → session_id
    → ConcurrentHandle tracks write_set, page_locks

Connection DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE via Phase 5B)
  → BtCursor.table_insert() / delete()
    → TransactionHandle.write_page(page_num, data)
      → concurrent_write_page(handle, lock_table, session_id, page, data)
        → InProcessPageLockTable.try_acquire(page, txn_id)
        → handle.write_set.insert(page, data)

Connection.execute_commit()
  → Pager.commit()
    → concurrent_commit(handle, commit_index, lock_table, session_id, seq)
      → validate_first_committer_wins(handle, commit_index) → FcwResult
      → If Clean: update CommitIndex, release all locks, mark committed
      → If Conflict: release locks, return MvccError::BusySnapshot

Connection READS during concurrent txn:
  → TransactionHandle.get_page(page_num)
    → concurrent_read_page(handle, page) → check local write set first
    → VersionStore.resolve(page, snapshot) → walk chain for visible version
```

## Dependency chain

5E depends on 5D (transaction lifecycle through pager) because concurrent writers need the
basic commit/rollback path working first. Within 5E:

- **5E.1** (BEGIN CONCURRENT wiring) is the foundation
- **5E.2** (page-level locking) depends on 5E.1
- **5E.3** (SSI validation) depends on 5E.2
- **5E.4** (BUSY_SNAPSHOT retry) depends on 5E.3
- **5E.5** (GC for old versions) depends on 5E.1 (independent of SSI path)
- **5E.6** (stress test) depends on 5E.4 and 5E.5 (needs everything working)

## Key types reference

- `ConcurrentHandle` — per-session state (snapshot, write_set, page_locks, state)
- `ConcurrentRegistry` — registry of active handles (max 128 concurrent writers)
- `InProcessPageLockTable` — 64-shard CacheAligned<Mutex<HashMap<PageNumber, TxnId>>>
- `SharedPageLockTable` — cross-process fixed-capacity open-addressing (1M entries, 12 MiB/table)
- `CommitIndex` — 64-shard RwLock<HashMap<PageNumber, CommitSeq>>
- `FcwResult` — Clean | Conflict { conflicting_pages, conflicting_commit_seq }
- `MvccError::Busy` — lock contention, `MvccError::BusySnapshot` — stale snapshot
- `SsiState` — rw-antidependency edges, pivot detection, marked_for_abort
- `GcScheduler` — frequency f_gc = clamp(pressure/target, f_min, f_max)
- `VersionStore` — arena + chain_heads HashMap<PageNumber, VersionIdx>
- `TransactionMode::Concurrent` vs `TransactionMode::Serialized`

## Children

- bd-14zc: 5E.1 — Wire BEGIN CONCURRENT through MVCC ConcurrentRegistry
- bd-kivg: 5E.2 — Enable MVCC page-level locking for write transactions
- bd-1xo1: 5E.3 — Wire SSI validation (SireadTable) at commit time
- bd-3uzh: 5E.4 — Implement SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT retry on page conflict
- bd-3bql: 5E.5 — Wire MVCC garbage collection for old page versions
- bd-1299: 5E.6 — Multi-threaded concurrent writer stress test

 {"id":"bd-1jup","title":"Repl: Design UDP packet format + replay/dup handling + auth_tag integration","description":"Design the on-wire packet format for fountain-coded replication (spec §3.4.2) and define replay/dup semantics.

## Required Fields
Each packet must allow the receiver to:
- identify which object this symbol belongs to
- validate integrity/auth before accepting
- place symbol in decode buffer

Suggested minimal header (versioned):
- protocol_magic + version
- `object_id` (changeset_id or ObjectId)
- `esi` (encoding symbol id)
- `k_source` and `r_repair` (or enough to derive)
- `symbol_size_T`
- `seed` (if needed for deterministic repair schedule)
- `payload_hash` (xxh3_128) and optional `auth_tag`

## Replay / Duplicate Handling
- Duplicates are common under lossy UDP; receiver must treat dup as no-op.
- Receiver may accept symbols in any order.
- Sender should prefer systematic ESIs first to enable “fast path” decode.

## Security (Optional, But Spec-Aligned)
- If auth enabled, invalid `auth_tag` means “treat as erasure” (do not poison decode).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Header is fixed-size, endian-defined, and has a compatibility story.
- Unit tests for encode/decode header + integrity checks.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:54:59.176426574Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:19:58.880330892Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T22:19:58.880308450Z","close_reason":"Completed: versioned fixed-size UDP packet header (v2), replay/dup semantics, auth-tag integrity handling, legacy compatibility, and encode/decode tests validated","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-1k6s","title":"5B.6: Remove hydration loop from open_storage_cursor for write cursors","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:45:13.583098456Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T02:26:51.327034155Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1k6s","depends_on_id":"bd-1soh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:42.394576713Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k6s","depends_on_id":"bd-24i5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:45:22.332947474Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k6s","depends_on_id":"bd-mpc9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:42.151925309Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k6s","depends_on_id":"bd-ujkm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:21:42.643295279Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1240,"issue_id":"bd-1k6s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# 5B.6: Remove hydration loop from open_storage_cursor for write cursors

## What

Currently, `open_storage_cursor()` in VdbeEngine has a "hydration" step: when opening a
B-tree cursor for writing, it copies ALL rows from MemTable into the B-tree cursor's pages.
This is because the B-tree starts empty (real pages aren't populated), so existing data must
be hydrated from MemDatabase.

Once CREATE TABLE allocates real root pages (5A.3) and data lives in the pager, this
hydration is unnecessary and harmful (it's O(N) per cursor open and wastes pager pages).

## Where to change

**Primary:** `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs` — `open_storage_cursor()` method

## Current code (conceptual)

```rust
fn open_storage_cursor(&mut self, cursor_id: i32, root_page: i32, writable: bool) -> bool {
    if let Some(txn_io) = &self.txn_page_io {
        let shared_io = txn_io.clone();

        // *** HYDRATION: Copy MemTable rows into B-tree pages ***
        // This creates B-tree pages from scratch using MemDatabase data
        if let Some(db) = &self.db {
            if let Some(table) = db.get_table(root_page) {
                // Write empty leaf-table page
                let empty_page = make_empty_leaf_page(PAGE_SIZE);
                shared_io.write_page(page_num, &empty_page)?;

                // Create cursor and insert all rows
                let mut cursor = BtCursor::new(shared_io.clone(), page_num, PAGE_SIZE, true);
                for row in &table.rows {
                    let record = encode_record(&row.values);
                    cursor.table_insert(&cx, row.rowid, &record)?;
                }
            }
        }

        let cursor = BtCursor::new(shared_io, page_num, PAGE_SIZE, true);
        self.storage_cursors.insert(cursor_id, StorageCursor { cursor, writable });
        true
    } else {
        false
    }
}
```

## Target code

```rust
fn open_storage_cursor(&mut self, cursor_id: i32, root_page: i32, writable: bool) -> bool {
    if let Some(txn_io) = &self.txn_page_io {
        let shared_io = txn_io.clone();
        let page_num = PageNumber::new(root_page as u32)
            .expect("root_page must be valid PageNumber");

        // NO HYDRATION — data already lives in pager from real B-tree operations
        // The root page was allocated by CREATE TABLE (5A.3) and rows inserted
        // by prior INSERT operations (5B.2). Just open the cursor.

        let cursor = BtCursor::new(shared_io, page_num, PAGE_SIZE, true /* is_table */);
        let backend = CursorBackend::Txn(cursor);
        self.storage_cursors.insert(cursor_id, StorageCursor {
            cursor: backend,
            cx: Cx::background(),
            writable,
            root_page,
        });
        true
    } else {
        false
    }
}
```

## Why this matters

The hydration loop is:
- O(N) in table size per cursor open — devastating for large tables
- Wasteful of pager pages (creates temporary B-tree that's thrown away)
- Incorrect once real pages exist (would overwrite committed data)
- A bottleneck for any write operation (every INSERT opens a cursor)

## Considerations

- This task MUST be done AFTER 5A.3 (real root page allocation) — otherwise there are
  no real pages to open the cursor on
- After this change, opening a cursor on a table that was never CREATE'd will fail
  (no root page exists). This is correct behavior.
- The MemPageStore variant of CursorBackend should remain for unit tests that don't
  want a full pager stack

## Tests

- `test_cursor_opens_without_hydration`: Open cursor, verify no extra pages written
- `test_cursor_reads_existing_btree_data`: INSERT via B-tree, close, reopen cursor, read
- `test_cursor_on_nonexistent_page_fails`: Open cursor on unallocated page → error
","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:47:24Z"},{"id":1303,"issue_id":"bd-1k6s","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# UPDATE: 5B.6 is now a late-stage "safety net removal" task

## Revised dependency position

**Original deps**: 5B.6 depends on 5B.1 (only)
**Revised deps**: 5B.6 depends on 5B.4, 5A.4, AND 5D.1

5B.6 (remove hydration loop) is now positioned as a **safety net removal** task that runs
AFTER verifying the new write + commit + schema-loading paths all work correctly.

## Why the dependency on 5D.1 (COMMIT through pager)?

The hydration loop in `open_storage_cursor()` (engine.rs) copies ALL rows from MemTable
into B-tree pages when a storage cursor is opened for writing. This is needed because
historically the B-tree was empty — data lived only in MemDatabase.

After Phase 5B (write-through), new data goes to the B-tree. But what about data that was
there BEFORE Phase 5B? The B-tree has it from the .db file (loaded by pager), but only if:
1. The .db file was written correctly (5D.1 — COMMIT through pager)
2. The schema was loaded from sqlite_master (5A.4 — schema loading on open)

Only after BOTH conditions are met is it safe to remove the hydration loop, because the
B-tree is guaranteed to have all data via the pager path.

## What to remove

In `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs`, the `open_storage_cursor()` function:

```rust
fn open_storage_cursor(&mut self, cursor_id: i32, root_page: u32, is_write: bool) -> Result<()> {
    // ... create StorageCursor ...

    // ===== THIS IS THE HYDRATION LOOP — REMOVE IT =====
    if is_write {
        if let Some(db) = &self.db {
            if let Some(table) = db.get_table_by_root_page(root_page) {
                for row in table.iter() {
                    storage_cursor.insert(row.rowid, &row.encode())?;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    // ===== END HYDRATION LOOP =====

    self.storage_cursors.insert(cursor_id, storage_cursor);
    Ok(())
}
```

## Verification before removal

Before removing the hydration loop:
1. Verify INSERT → COMMIT → close → reopen → SELECT returns same data (5B.2 + 5D.1 + 5A.4)
2. Verify CREATE TABLE → INSERT → COMMIT → close → reopen → INSERT more → data consistent
3. Run regression guard (5F.1) after removal

## Updated tests (with logging)

```
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=insert] rows=100 via=btree_write_through
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=commit] via=pager_commit
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=reopen] path={path}
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=select] rows=100 via=storage_cursor ✓
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=verify] all data matches original ✓
```

- `test_no_hydration_data_survives_reopen`: INSERT, COMMIT, reopen → data via pager (no hydration)
- `test_no_hydration_write_cursor_starts_empty`: open_storage_cursor for write → cursor positioned at start, no pre-populated data
- `test_no_hydration_regression_guard`: Run full test suite after removal → 0 regressions
","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:26:51Z"}]}

## What

Currently, `open_storage_cursor()` in VdbeEngine has a "hydration" step: when opening a
B-tree cursor for writing, it copies ALL rows from MemTable into the B-tree cursor's pages.
This is because the B-tree starts empty (real pages aren't populated), so existing data must

## Current code (conceptual)

```rust
fn open_storage_cursor(&mut self, cursor_id: i32, root_page: i32, writable: bool) -> bool {
    if let Some(txn_io) = &self.txn_page_io {
        let shared_io = txn_io.clone();

        // *** HYDRATION: Copy MemTable rows into B-tree pages ***
        // This creates B-tree pages from scratch using MemDatabase data
        if let Some(db) = &self.db {
            if let Some(table) = db.get_table(root_page) {
                // Write empty leaf-table page
                let empty_page = make_empty_leaf_page(PAGE_SIZE);
                shared_io.write_page(page_num, &empty_page)?;

                // Create cursor and insert all rows
                let mut cursor = BtCursor::new(shared_io.clone(), page_num, PAGE_SIZE, true);
                for row in &table.rows {
                    let record = encode_record(&row.values);
                    cursor.table_insert(&cx, row.rowid, &record)?;
                }
            }
        }

        let cursor = BtCursor::new(shared_io, page_num, PAGE_SIZE, true);
        self.storage_cursors.insert(cursor_id, StorageCursor { cursor, writable });
        true
    } else {
        false
    }
}
```

## Target code

```rust
fn open_storage_cursor(&mut self, cursor_id: i32, root_page: i32, writable: bool) -> bool {
    if let Some(txn_io) = &self.txn_page_io {
        let shared_io = txn_io.clone();
        let page_num = PageNumber::new(root_page as u32)
            .expect("root_page must be valid PageNumber");

        // by prior INSERT operations (5B.2). Just open the cursor.

        let cursor = BtCursor::new(shared_io, page_num, PAGE_SIZE, true /* is_table */);
        let backend = CursorBackend::Txn(cursor);
        self.storage_cursors.insert(cursor_id, StorageCursor {
            cursor: backend,
            cx: Cx::background(),
            writable,
            root_page,
        });
        true
    } else {
        false
    }
}
```

## Why this matters

The hydration loop is:
- O(N) in table size per cursor open — devastating for large tables
- Wasteful of pager pages (creates temporary B-tree that's thrown away)
- Incorrect once real pages exist (would overwrite committed data)
- A bottleneck for any write operation (every INSERT opens a cursor)

## Considerations

- This task MUST be done AFTER 5A.3 (real root page allocation) — otherwise there are

- After this change, opening a cursor on a table that was never CREATE'd will fail

- The MemPageStore variant of CursorBackend should remain for unit tests that don't
  want a full pager stack

- `test_cursor_reads_existing_btree_data`: INSERT via B-tree, close, reopen cursor, read

## Updated tests (with logging)

```
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=insert] rows=100 via=btree_write_through
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=commit] via=pager_commit
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=reopen] path={path}
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=select] rows=100 via=storage_cursor ✓
[5B6][test=no_hydration][step=verify] all data matches original ✓
```

- `test_no_hydration_data_survives_reopen`: INSERT, COMMIT, reopen → data via pager (no hydration)
- `test_no_hydration_write_cursor_starts_empty`: open_storage_cursor for write → cursor positioned at start, no pre-populated data
- `test_no_hydration_regression_guard`: Run full test suite after removal → 0 regressions

 {"id":"bd-1k8e","title":"Hybrid: Tests + perf checks (fast path cheap; fallback explainable)","description":"Add tests and perf checks that lock in hybrid decode behavior.

## Tests
- Fast path succeeds when all systematic symbols are present and valid.
- Fast path aborts on:
  - missing symbol
  - hash mismatch
  - auth_tag failure
- Fallback decode succeeds when enough symbols exist (including repairs).
- Fallback decode fails beyond budget and emits explainable failure artifacts.

## Perf Checks
- Microbench or sampled measurement that shows:
  - fast path does not invoke decoder
  - fast path overhead is effectively constant

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deterministic test suite; no flaky probability claims.
- Evidence/proof output is stable for snapshot diffs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:56:24.829726929Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:42:52.929571892Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T22:42:52.929552425Z","close_reason":"Added hybrid fast-path/fallback test matrix (missing/hash/auth aborts, fallback success/failure + proof stability) and perf checks via symbol_ops benchmarks","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1k8e","depends_on_id":"bd-35bv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:57:06.700290657Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1k8e","depends_on_id":"bd-xpbo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:57:06.890755373Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-1kqz","title":"PARITY-B4: Handle explicit column lists with IPK (present, absent, reordered)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:51:31.131965234Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T02:23:33.096826390Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T02:23:33.096807464Z","close_reason":"Implemented column list remapping for IPK index. Maps from table-schema position to VALUES position.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1kqz","depends_on_id":"bd-13b8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:51:55.493028617Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1265,"issue_id":"bd-1kqz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# PARITY-B4: Handle explicit column lists with IPK (present, absent, reordered)

## Context

INSERT statements can specify explicit column lists that differ from the schema order:

```sql
CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER);

-- Case 1: All columns, schema order (handled by B1)
INSERT INTO t VALUES (42, 'Alice', 30);

-- Case 2: All columns, reordered
INSERT INTO t(age, name, id) VALUES (30, 'Alice', 42);

-- Case 3: IPK omitted (should auto-generate)
INSERT INTO t(name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30);

-- Case 4: Only IPK specified
INSERT INTO t(id) VALUES (42);
```

## The Problem

`ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx` contains the IPK column's index in the **schema** (e.g., 0 for `id`
as the first column). But when an explicit column list reorders or omits columns, the IPK's
position in the **VALUES list** differs from its schema position.

## What to Change

**File**: `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs`
**Location**: Within `codegen_insert_values()` and `codegen_insert_select()`

The fix must compute a `values_ipk_idx: Option<usize>` — the position of the IPK column
within the VALUES list (or None if the IPK is omitted from the column list):

```rust
let values_ipk_idx = if stmt.columns.is_empty() {
    // No explicit column list — schema order, so IPK is at schema position
    ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx
} else {
    // Explicit column list — find IPK column name within the list
    ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx.and_then(|schema_idx| {
        let ipk_name = &table.columns[schema_idx].name;
        stmt.columns.iter().position(|col| col.eq_ignore_ascii_case(ipk_name))
    })
};
```

Then use `values_ipk_idx` instead of `ctx.rowid_alias_col_idx` in the IPK routing logic.

If `values_ipk_idx` is None (IPK omitted from column list), always use NewRowid.

## Edge Cases

1. **Case-insensitive matching**: Column names should be compared case-insensitively
   (SQLite is case-insensitive for identifiers).

2. **IPK at non-zero position**: `INSERT INTO t(name, id) VALUES('x', 42)` — IPK is at
   VALUES index 1, not schema index 0. The routing logic must use VALUES index.

3. **DEFAULT for non-IPK columns**: When IPK is specified but other columns are omitted,
   those columns get their DEFAULT values (or NULL). This is existing behavior and shouldn't
   be affected by the IPK fix.

4. **Interaction with B1 NULL handling**: If IPK is in the column list but gets a NULL value,
   the B3 NULL fallback logic applies.

## Current State of Column List Handling

Need to verify how `codegen_insert_values` currently handles explicit column lists. If it
already remaps VALUES positions to schema positions before calling emit_expr, the remapping
may need to happen before or after that step. Read the actual code to determine.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `INSERT INTO t(age, name, id) VALUES (30, 'Alice', 42); SELECT id FROM t` → 42
- `INSERT INTO t(name, age) VALUES ('Alice', 30); SELECT id FROM t` → auto-generated
- `INSERT INTO t(id) VALUES (42); SELECT * FROM t` → (42, NULL, NULL)
- `INSERT INTO t(name) VALUES ('Alice'); SELECT id FROM t` → auto-generated
- No regressions in existing column-list INSERT tests

## Dependency

Blocked by B1 (bd-13b8) — the basic IPK routing must work first. This task extends it
to handle the column-list edge cases.
","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:51:55Z"},{"id":1294,"issue_id":"bd-1kqz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# B4 Implementation Complete (QuietBeaver, Session 22)

## Change Summary

Fixed `codegen_insert()` in `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs` to handle explicit
column lists with IPK:

When `INSERT INTO t(name, id) VALUES ('test', 42)` is used:
1. `rowid_alias_col_idx` is `Some(0)` (IPK is column 0 in table schema)
2. But in the VALUES list, id is at position 1 (column list order: name, id)
3. New code computes `effective_ctx` by looking up the IPK column name in `stmt.columns`
4. If IPK column is present in column list: remap index to its VALUES position
5. If IPK column is absent from column list: set `rowid_alias_col_idx = None` (auto-generate)

## Test Results
- All 205 vdbe tests: PASS
- All 156 fsqlite tests: PASS
- All 833 fsqlite-core tests: PASS
